categories.title: SOUNDS LIKE HISTORY

The 1839 novel 'The Charterhouse of Parma,' a sweeping tale of Italian intrigue and passion, was written by this French author who used a single-word pen name.

The Bangles' 1986 hit 'Manic Monday' was secretly penned by this Minneapolis musical genius under the pseudonym Christopher.

This South American country is home to the Pantanal, the world's largest tropical wetland, spanning an area larger than many European nations.

Jiles Perry Richardson, a DJ and singer who died in the 1959 plane crash also known as 'The Day the Music Died,' was more commonly known by this colorful stage name.

categories.title: BODY OF KNOWLEDGE

clues.clue: The (+) or (–) marker appended to your blood type, such as O-positive, takes its name from this primate whose blood was used in its discovery.
Doctors use this two-letter abbreviation to name an infectious lung disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
When a diabetic's blood sugar dips dangerously low, a physician would record this condition on their chart.
Skeletal muscle is built from bundles of long, thin structures called these, whose name derives from the Greek for 'muscle thread'.
Saturn's moon shares a name with this scientific concept: the branch of biology concerned with synthesizing or studying life-forms that are not of terrestrial origin.

This Japanese author's novel 'Norwegian Wood' takes its title from a Beatles song and follows a college student mourning a lost friend in 1960s Tokyo.
The beloved Chinese wuxia novel 'The Return of the Condor Heroes' was penned under a pen name by this author, whose real name was Louis Cha.

In Greek mythology, the god Zeus took the form of this animal in order to seduce the Phoenician princess Europa.
This old Scottish word, meaning to chase girls around haystacks after dark, sounds more pastoral than scandalous.

This psychological effect describes the tendency of unskilled individuals to overestimate their own competence due to their inability to recognize their own limitations.
More formally known as dihydrogen monoxide, this compound makes up roughly 60% of the adult human body.
C6H12O6 is the molecular formula for this simple sugar that serves as the primary energy source for living cells.

The Roman Emperor who expanded the empire to its greatest-ever territorial extent, pushing into Mesopotamia and Dacia.

finals.title: AVIATION FIRSTS
finals.clue: On July 25, 1909, this French aviator completed a historic 37-minute flight across the English Channel, landing near Dover Castle and winning a £1,000 prize from the Daily Mail.

finals.title: ART HISTORY
finals.clue: This French Post-Impressionist painter developed the technique of Pointillism and spent two years applying millions of tiny dots of color to complete his monumental 1886 canvas depicting Parisian leisure on a riverbank.

finals.title: SCIENCE & DISCOVERY
finals.clue: Though often credited with many astronomical achievements, this Italian scientist is also recognized as the inventor of the telescope, a claim that remains debated but is attached to his name in popular history.

finals.title: MATHEMATICAL CURVES
finals.clue: This plane curve, defined by the equation x³ + y³ = 3axy, was devised by a French mathematician specifically to challenge a rival's techniques for finding extrema, and bears that mathematician's name.

finals.title: ANCIENT SCRIPTURE
finals.clue: Comprising 700 verses across 18 chapters, this Sanskrit text records a philosophical conversation on the battlefield of Kurukshetra between a warrior prince and his divine charioteer, and forms part of the epic Mahabharata.

finals.title: LOST KINGDOMS
finals.clue: Before gaining its modern name, this Southeast Asian country was known for centuries as 'Lan Xang,' meaning 'Land of a Million Elephants,' and was a powerful kingdom along the Mekong River.

finals.title: INK & PAPER TRAIL
finals.clue: This British civil servant and reformer, who worked in the Post Office in the 1830s, is widely credited with inventing the adhesive postage stamp and establishing the foundation of the modern postal system.

finals.title: LITERARY LEGACY
finals.clue: This author, the son of J.R.R. Tolkien, spent decades editing and publishing his father's unfinished manuscripts, including the 1980 volume 'Unfinished Tales of Númenor and Middle-earth'.

finals.title: PRESIDENTIAL FIRSTS
finals.clue: All of his predecessors were born in one of the original thirteen colonies, but this 16th president — born in a Kentucky log cabin — broke that mold when he took office in 1861.

finals.title: LITERARY LEGACIES
finals.clue: Published in two parts — 1678 and 1684 — this allegorical dream narrative by English preacher John Bunyan follows a character named Christian on his journey from the City of Destruction to the Celestial City, and remains one of the best-selling books in the English language.
